title: Critical Hoelder exponents
speaker: Camillo De Lellis
abstract: In a series of works with Laszlo Szekelyhidi Jr. we pointed out an unusual analogy between two problems in rather distant areas: a long standing conjecture of Onsager in the theory of turbulence and a (less known) critical regularity problem in classical differential geometry. In both cases the issue is to find the critical regularity above which the solutions to a certain system of partial differential equations are ``well-behaved'' and below which they are instead rather ``wild''.

proof_stages.label: Euler balance-law and energy setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the incompressible Euler equations, rewrites them as local balance laws for mass and momentum, defines weak solutions through those balances, and recalls energy conservation for smooth solutions under natural boundary conditions.

proof_stages.label: Onsager's critical Hölder conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Onsager's conjecture is stated as a threshold at spatial Hölder exponent 1/3: above it weak Euler solutions conserve kinetic energy, while below it there should be nonconservative, in particular dissipative, solutions.

proof_stages.label: Turbulence motivation and Onsager theorem status
proof_stages.summary: The 1/3 threshold is motivated by the Navier-Stokes dissipative anomaly and Kolmogorov scaling, and the known resolution of Onsager's conjecture is summarized from Constantin-E-Titi through convex integration constructions reaching the sharp exponent.

proof_stages.label: Relaxed Euler equations and h-principle flexibility
proof_stages.summary: Starting from Shnirelman's nonconservative weak solutions and the De Lellis-Székelyhidi refinement, the argument identifies weak limits of smooth Euler flows with Reynolds-stress errors and formulates an h-principle: relaxed Euler solutions can be approximated by exact weak solutions.

proof_stages.label: Nash-Kuiper and Borisov as the geometric analogue
proof_stages.summary: The Euler flexibility mechanism is connected to differential inclusions and then to the Nash-Kuiper theorem for C^1 isometric embeddings, whose flexibility is contrasted with C^2 curvature rigidity and with Borisov's C^{1,α} partial thresholds.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural 1/2 threshold for isometric immersions
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by the Onsager analogy, the talk formulates the conjecture that C^{1,α} isometric immersions of positively curved two-surfaces are rigid for α>1/2 and Nash-Kuiper flexible for α<1/2, then reviews the best known bounds.

proof_stages.label: Boundary geodesic-curvature threshold theorem
proof_stages.summary: A new theorem with Dominik Inauen is presented: for prescribed-boundary isometric immersions, the smooth geodesic-curvature boundary constraint persists at C^{1,1/2+ε}, while below 1/2 high-codimension C^{1,1/2−ε} embeddings can violate the corresponding tangent-angle constraint.

examples.name: One-dimensional eikonal zigzags
examples.note: The equation |∇w|=1 is reduced to one-dimensional functions with slopes ±1, whose rapid zigzags uniformly approximate functions with smaller slope, illustrating the relaxation/convexity mechanism later compared with Euler.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the 1D eikonal equation by noting slopes must be ±1, drawing zigzags, and explaining how such exact solutions approximate functions with smaller slope

passes.reason: A one-dimensional ±1-slope eikonal toy model is used to show exact solutions can converge to a relaxed |w'|<1 limit.

examples.name: Fixed-length curves converging to a shorter curve
examples.note: A curve with many small zigzags can have fixed length while converging to a curve that shortens distances, making the Nash-Kuiper short-map phenomenon visually concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through a sequence of same-length curves with increasingly fine zigzags whose uniform limit has strictly shorter length

passes.reason: An ordinary zigzagging sequence of equal-length curves is used to show uniform limits need not preserve length.
ordinary zigzagging curves of fixed length are used to show that length need not be preserved under uniform convergence.
Ordinary oscillatory fixed-length curves are used to show length preservation can fail under uniform convergence.
The example involves length collapsing in the limit, and is used to show uniform limits of length-preserving curves need not preserve length.
ordinary fixed-length zigzag curves are used to show that a uniform limit can have smaller length, so length preservation is not closed
Wrinkled curves of fixed length are used to show that a uniform limit need not preserve length and may be shorter.
The zigzagging fixed-length curves are used to show that length need not be preserved under uniform limits, while the curves themselves are otherwise ordinary.
The oscillating fixed-length curves are used to show that length preservation can fail in the limit, but the curves themselves are not structurally special.
The zigzagging fixed-length curves illustrate that length can drop under uniform convergence, defeating preservation of length in the limit.
The curves illustrate that a uniform limit of length-preserving curves can be shorter, refuting naive preservation of length in the limit.
Used to show length is not preserved under mere uniform convergence, but the curves themselves are ordinary oscillatory curves.
The oscillating fixed-length curves are structurally ordinary, but are used to show that uniform limits of length-preserving curves may have strictly shorter length.

examples.name: Round sphere squeezed into a tiny ball
examples.note: Applying Nash-Kuiper to a round sphere mapped into a small sphere of radius ε illustrates the dramatic contrast between C^1 wrinkled flexibility and C^2 convex rigidity.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies Nash–Kuiper to the unit round sphere mapped into an epsilon ball and contrasts the resulting wrinkled C1 immersion with the convexity/area obstruction for C2 immersions

passes.reason: The maximally symmetric round sphere crumpled into arbitrarily small balls gives a C1 failure of the C2 convexity/rigidity picture.
the intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric and isometrically crumpled into arbitrarily small extrinsic diameter, showing C2 convexity/rigidity fails at C1 regularity.
The intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric and is pushed to arbitrarily tiny extrinsic diameter, refuting C2-style rigidity at C1 regularity.

The intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric and isometrically packed into an arbitrarily tiny ball as an extreme wrinkled C1 object, showing C2-style convexity/rigidity fails at low regularity.
the intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric and is squeezed to arbitrarily tiny extrinsic size, showing C1 isometries evade the usual C2 convex rigidity
The intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric, and its Nash–Kuiper squeezing into an arbitrarily tiny ball is an extreme violation of C2 convex rigidity.
A maximally symmetric round sphere is put by Nash–Kuiper into an arbitrarily small extrinsic ball, a limiting extreme that contradicts C2-style rigidity expectations.
A maximally symmetric round sphere is put in an arbitrarily small extrinsic ball, illustrating failure of C2-style rigidity for C1 isometries.
A C¹ isometric immersion of the intrinsically round sphere into an arbitrarily tiny ball is an extreme crumpling example used to refute C²-style rigidity at C¹ regularity.
A maximally symmetric round sphere is C1-isometrically crumpled into an arbitrarily small ball, contradicting smooth rigidity intuition and giving an extreme extrinsic compression.
Used to refute smooth convex-rigidity intuition at C1 regularity; the intrinsically round sphere is maximally symmetric and is placed in an arbitrarily small-diameter limiting regime.
